reasoning:
The forecast reference class is stock price movement over horizons varying from one week to one year. Stock returns are typically modeled as a random walk with a very slight positive drift over long periods, but recent volatility dominates short-term predictions. We pulled the yfinance history up to March 14, 2025 (the most recent trading day prior to March 16), which showed a close of ~80.06 following a steady drop from ~86 over the preceding weeks. The fetch_ts_yfinance tool calculated a combined probability of 0.44 for a short-term increase, weighted by negative short-term momentum and historical seasonal data showing weakness around this late-March period. Consequently, my anchor for the shortest horizon (March 23) is 0.44. 

Per the system instruction, forecast uncertainty must increase over greater horizons, demanding that the probabilities move closer to 0.50 as the resolution date extends. I scale the probabilities linearly toward 0.50 for the subsequent dates: 1-month (0.46), 3-months (0.48), 6-months (0.49), and 1-year (0.50). This strictly satisfies the condition that distant forecasts approach the maximum uncertainty bound of 50%.
